title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, caste, color, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported community leaders responsible enforcement sbyrum@uams.edu. complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly. community leaders obligated respect privacy security reporter incident.

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: install proteoDA v1.0 GitHub (dependencies vignettes built):
code: # Install proteoDA version 1.0.0 # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("ByrumLab/proteoDA@v1.0.0",                         dependencies = TRUE,                         build_vignettes = TRUE,                         force = TRUE)

what: Load the package and view the available vignettes:
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
code: library(proteoDA) # browseVignettes(package = "proteoDA")

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: proteoDA ships small DIA-style test datasets quick evaluation. Three data matrix required input: protein intensity data - proteins rows samples columns sample metadata - links sample columns protein intensity sample group information protein annotation - includes uniprot_id required column, protein description, gene names, protein information. NOTE: 1. protein intensity dataframe must include column labeled uniprot_id. becomes rownames lists R must unique values row. 2. sample metadata csv file column labeled sample matches colnames samples protein intensity dataframe.

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: protein data files generated various database search engines include protein annotation along protein intensities sample file. order create DAList proteoDA, need split database search results protein annotation intensity data. columns needed analysis included.
code: annotation_data <- input_data[, 1:4]            # protein-level annotations intensity_data  <- input_data[, 5:ncol(input_data)]  # quantitative sample columns  # Ensure metadata rownames match intensity column names rownames(sample_metadata) <- sample_metadata$data_column_name

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: DAList central container class proteoDA holding intensity, metadata, downstream analysis results.
code: raw <- DAList( data      = intensity_data, annotation= annotation_data, metadata  = sample_metadata )

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: filter_samples() allows remove outlier samples, pooled QC samples used limma group comparisons.
code: # samples with the group named "Pool" will be removed  filtered_samples <- filter_samples(raw, group != "Pool")

what: How proteoDA v1.0 deals with missing values
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: Depending experimental design, may wish treat intensity values detection limit instrument missing data (NA) value 0 intensity (protein truly knocked ). Though can done manually importing data DAList object, also provide simple utility functions convert missing values 0 vice-verse. missing_to_zero() - converts missing values (NA default, user-provided values) 0 - mainly downstream export/compatibility tools require fully numeric matrix, model fitting, performing PCA analysis zero_to_missing() - converts 0s NA - limma treats missing rather true measurements. - NA values required filter_proteins_by_group() missing value heatmap

previous_headings: Preprocessing > How proteoDA v1.0 deals with missing values
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: way NA vs 0 handled exactly limma NA values expression matrix treated missing. protein/feature, lmFit(): 1. drops samples NA feature , 2. fits linear model using remaining non-missing samples. means: - Different proteins can fit different numbers samples. - whole group (e.g. “cancer” samples) NA protein, corresponding design column / contrast becomes non-estimable limma returns NA logFC, t-statistic, p-value protein/contrast. - eBayes() uses available residual degrees freedom per protein, proteins many missing values borrow information still appropriately smaller df.

previous_headings: Preprocessing > How proteoDA v1.0 deals with missing values
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: value 0 just number limma. log2 scale, 0 means “intensity 1”; raw scale means “exactly zero”. lmFit() eBayes() assume zeros real observed values includes mean variance calculations, allows contribute fold-changes t-statistics. zeros actually mean “detected / missing” rather true measured abundance, treating real values can: Pull group means strongly downward, Inflate log2 fold-changes one group many zeros doesn’t, Artificially increase within-group variance “mixed” features (real intensities, zeros), Distort moderated variance estimates eBayes(). NOTE: NA = “measurement available; ignore protein’s model fit”. limma never tries impute internally. proteoDA v1.0 include imputation method; however, version 2.0 now includes imputation using normal distribution performed Perseus Software (Cox lab). Recommendataion: Convert explicit zeros NA (recommended filtering):
code: filtered_samples <- zero_to_missing(filtered_samples)

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: Filter features based replicate presence (e.g., ≥4 values ≥1 group): function used remove proteins DAList, filtering proteins based levels missing values “data” data frame DAList. grouping_column must column metadata DAList, lists group membership sample. min_reps min_groups arguments determine number replicates/samples per group (min_reps) number groups (min_groups) protein must non-missing intensity values order retained. function assumes missing values encoded NA. Rule Thumb require least 2/3 replicates values least one sample group.
code: filtered_proteins <- filter_proteins_by_group( filtered_samples, min_reps        = 4, min_groups      = 1, grouping_column = "group" )

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: write_norm_report() saves PDF report containing variety qualitative plots give information performance different normalization methods. report useful choosing normalization method use downstream analysis.

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: Proteomics intensity data almost always contain systematic, non-biological biases—arising sample handling, instrument drift, batch effects, uncontrolled sources. emphasized proteiNorm publication (Graw et al), selecting appropriate normalization strategy critical preprocessing step poor normalization can obscure true biological changes, inflate variance, introduce artificial fold-changes. support evaluation proteoDA, function write_norm_report() provides automated, reproducible way compare performance eight widely used normalization methods. Internally, wrapper runs QC, normalization, diagnostic steps implemented proteiNorm framework, produces static PDF HTML report suitable documentation publication.

previous_headings: Normalization and QC > Evaluating Normalization Methods with write_norm_report()
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: write_norm_report() applies following methods protein abundance matrix Log2 transformation (log2) Median normalization (median) Mean normalization (mean) Variance Stabilizing Normalization (vsn) Quantile normalization (quantile) Cyclic loess normalization (cycloess) Robust linear regression (rlr) Global intensity scaling (gi) methods represent combination microarray-derived normalization strategies proteomics-specific regressions/scaling approaches, many consistently rank among top performers systematic evaluations label-free proteomics data.

previous_headings: Normalization and QC > Evaluating Normalization Methods with write_norm_report()
what: What diagnostics does the report generate?
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: Following proteiNorm workflow, proteoDA report evaluates method consistent QC panel: Total intensity boxplots: Checks whether global sample intensities become comparable normalization. PCA plots: Visualizes grouping structure, batch effects, separation biological conditions. Missing value heatmap: Displays patterns missingness (MAR vs MNAR) clustered heatmap. Intragroup variation metrics normalization method, report quantifies: - PCV (pooled coefficient variation) - PEV (pooled estimate variance) - PMAD (pooled median absolute deviation) metrics key evaluation criteria proteiNorm lower intragroup variation indicates stable, better-performing normalization method. Correlation metrics: Boxplots within-group correlations Full Pearson correlation heatmaps Higher intragroup correlation suggests biological replicates align normalization. Log2 ratio distributions: Distribution pairwise log2 fold-changes sample groups, expected center around zero unless strong directed regulation known, 2-fold spike study.

previous_headings: Normalization and QC > Evaluating Normalization Methods with write_norm_report()
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: goal choose normalization method : - Minimizes within-group variation (PCV/PEV/PMAD) - Maximizes correlation among replicates - Produces centered log2-ratio distributions - Yields PCA plots expected grouping minimal batch effects - Shows stable, comparable total intensities demonstrated proteiNorm paper’s case studies (mouse, breast cancer, spike-datasets) , different experiments may favor different methods—VSN cyclic loess often rank highly, single method optimal designs. write_norm_report() makes comparisons reproducible proteoDA workflow. Reference: Graw, S., Tang, J., Zafar, M. K., Byrd, . K., Bolden, C., Peterson, E. C., & Byrum, S. D. (2020). proteiNorm – User-Friendly Tool Normalization Analysis TMT Label-Free Protein Quantification. ACS Omega, 5(40), 25625-25633. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsomega.0c02564
code: write_norm_report( filtered_proteins, grouping_column = "group", output_dir = "01_QC_report_v1.0", filename = "normalization.pdf", overwrite = TRUE )  # Available methods: "log2", "median", "mean", "vsn", "quantile", "cycloess", "rlr", "gi"  normalized <- normalize_data(filtered_proteins, norm_method = "cycloess")

what: 9. Advanced: 2×2 Factorial Design (Intercept Model with Interaction)
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: example demonstrates 2×2 factorial design interaction using core v1.0 API . assume metadata two factors: cell levels WT, HET (genotype) treat levels Sham, Gy (treatment) Note: proteoDA simplifies design-matrix column names (e.g., Intercept, HET, Gy, HET.treatGy) contrasts plot labels cleaner.

previous_headings: 9. Advanced: 2×2 Factorial Design (Intercept Model with Interaction)
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
code: ## ---- nine_setup_factors, echo=TRUE ------------------------------------------ # Ensure the factors exist in the metadata and set reference levels stopifnot(all(c("cell", "treat") %in% names(sample_metadata)))  normalized$metadata$cell  <- factor(normalized$metadata$cell,  levels = c("WT","HET")) normalized$metadata$treat <- factor(normalized$metadata$treat, levels = c("Sham","Gy"))

previous_headings: 9. Advanced: 2×2 Factorial Design (Intercept Model with Interaction)

code: ## ---- nine_show_contrasts_table, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE ------------------- # Render a small table of the contrasts we will use (for the vignette) contrasts_df <- data.frame(   Label = c(     "WT_Gy_vs_HET_Gy",     "WT_Sham_vs_HET_Sham",     "TreatEffect_WT",     "TreatEffect_HET",     "Diff_in_effect_WT_HET",     "Avg_Treat_Gy_vs_Sham_unscaled"   ),   Definition = c(     "(Intercept + Gy) - (Intercept + HET + Gy + HET.treatGy)",     "Intercept - (Intercept + HET)",     "Gy",     "Gy + HET.treatGy",     "Gy - (Gy + HET.treatGy)",     "(Intercept + Gy) + (Intercept + HET + Gy + HET.treatGy) - (Intercept) - (Intercept + HET)"   ),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  knitr::kable(contrasts_df, caption = "Example contrasts for 2×2 factorial (intercept model)")

code: ## ---- nine_write_contrasts_csv, echo=TRUE ------------------------------------ # Save the exact same contrasts to a CSV that add_contrasts() can read # (a simple one-contrast-per-line plain text file also works; here we use writeLines for clarity) fac_con_lines <- c(   "WT_Gy_vs_HET_Gy = (Intercept + Gy) - (Intercept + HET + Gy + HET.treatGy)",   "WT_Sham_vs_HET_Sham = Intercept - (Intercept + HET)",   "TreatEffect_WT = Gy",   "TreatEffect_HET = Gy + HET.treatGy",   "Diff_in_effect_WT_HET = Gy - (Gy + HET.treatGy)",   "Avg_Treat_Gy_vs_Sham_unscaled = (Intercept + Gy) + (Intercept + HET + Gy + HET.treatGy) - (Intercept) - (Intercept + HET)" ) writeLines(fac_con_lines, "contrasts_factorial_intercept.csv")

previous_headings: 9. Advanced: 2×2 Factorial Design (Intercept Model with Interaction) > 9.3 Add design and contrasts for an interaction model (two cell types and two treatments).
what: Two-factor encoding: ~ treat1 * treat2 includes a main effect and an interaction term; the : column quantifies interaction (synergy or repression).
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
code: # Add factorial design with interaction (intercept model) fac <- add_design(   normalized,   design_formula = ~ cell * treat )  # Add contrasts from the CSV we just wrote fac <- add_contrasts(   fac,   contrasts_file = "contrasts_factorial_intercept.csv" )  # (Optional sanity check) colnames(fac$design$design_matrix) # Expected: "Intercept", "HET", "Gy", "HET.treatGy"

what: 9.5 Interpretation of the Interaction model ~ cell * treat
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: Interpretation tips TreatEffect_WT Gy vs Sham effect within WT (reference genotype). TreatEffect_HET Gy vs Sham effect within HET (WT treatment effect + interaction). Diff_in_effect_WT_HET compares treatment effects genotypes (interaction contrast). Avg_Treat_Gy_vs_Sham_unscaled sum two genotype-specific treatment effects. need literal average, divide logFC 2 fact downstream step (see proteoDA version 2.0 tutorial)

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: section illustrates two approaches handle non-independent samples proteoDA v1.0: Paired fixed-effect model: explicitly include pair_id term design (~ 0 + group + pair_id). Blocking duplicateCorrelation: set DAList$design$random_factor blocking column name calling fit_limma_model(). function estimates intra-block correlation internally applies limma fit. ✅ Use fixed-effect approach number pairs modest want strict within-pair comparisons. ✅ Use duplicateCorrelation many features repeated measures (efficient robust omics).

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: Design: ~ 0 + group + pair_id pair gets coefficient. contrasts compare groups within pairs.
code: ## ---- ten_paired_fixed_effect_setup, echo=TRUE -------------------------------- # Requirements in metadata: #  - a grouping variable used in your comparison, e.g. "group" with levels {Pre, Post} #  - a pairing variable, e.g. "pair_id" (or "subject"), repeated for matched samples  # Metadata must contain both "group" and "pair_id" stopifnot(all(c("group","pair_id") %in% names(normalized$metadata)))  # Ensure factors (and set reference levels if needed) normalized$metadata$group   <- factor(normalized$metadata$group)         # e.g., c("Pre","Post") normalized$metadata$pair_id <- factor(normalized$metadata$pair_id)  # Add paired fixed-effect design paired_fe <- add_design(   normalized,   design_formula = ~ 0 + group + pair_id )  # Contrasts: Post vs Pre (labels show up in plots) paired_fe <- add_contrasts(   paired_fe,   contrasts_vector = "Post_vs_Pre = Post - Pre" )  # Fit and extract fit_paired_fe <- fit_limma_model(paired_fe)  res_paired_fe <- extract_DA_results(   fit_paired_fe,   pval_thresh       = 0.05,   lfc_thresh        = 0.5,   adj_method        = "BH",   extract_intercept = FALSE )  # Write outputs write_limma_tables(   res_paired_fe,   output_dir = "10A_Paired_FixedEffect",   overwrite  = TRUE,   add_filter = TRUE )  write_limma_plots(   res_paired_fe,   grouping_column = "group",   table_columns   = c("uniprot_id","protein_info"),   title_column    = "uniprot_id",   output_dir      = "10A_Paired_FixedEffect",   tmp_subdir      = "tmp",   height          = 1000,   width           = 1000 )

previous_headings: 10. Paired / Blocking Designs (v1.0 Functions) > 10.1 Paired (fixed-effect) model
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: absorbs baseline differences per pair via fixed effect (pair_id defined sample_metadata.csv). Use every pair conditions (e.g., Pre/Post) number pairs huge.

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: Design: ~ 0 + group Random factor: assign blocking column name DAList$design$random_factor. fit_limma_model() : Estimate consensus correlation via duplicateCorrelation() Inform user estimated value Apply automatically model fit. limma estimates intra-block correlation uses model fit—computationally efficient close spirit random intercept.
code: ## ---- ten_blocking_dupCor_setup, echo=TRUE ----------------------------------- # Requirements in metadata: #  - a grouping variable (e.g. "group" with levels {Pre, Post}) #  - a blocking variable (e.g. "pair_id" or "subject"), repeated for correlated samples  stopifnot(all(c("group","pair_id") %in% names(normalized$metadata)))  # Factor coding (reference choice doesn't matter for no-intercept designs) normalized$metadata$group   <- factor(normalized$metadata$group) normalized$metadata$pair_id <- factor(normalized$metadata$pair_id)  # Add simple group-means design (no intercept) blocked <- add_design(   normalized,   design_formula = ~ 0 + group )  # Contrast: Post vs Pre blocked <- add_contrasts(   blocked,   contrasts_vector = "Post_vs_Pre = Post - Pre" )  # Specify the blocking variable for duplicateCorrelation blocked$design$random_factor <- "pair_id"  # Fit the model; duplicateCorrelation will run automatically fit_blocked <- fit_limma_model(blocked)  res_blocked <- extract_DA_results(   fit_blocked,   pval_thresh       = 0.05,   lfc_thresh        = 0.5,   adj_method        = "BH",   extract_intercept = FALSE )  # Write outputs write_limma_tables(   res_blocked,   output_dir = "10B_Blocked_dupCor",   overwrite  = TRUE,   add_filter = TRUE )  write_limma_plots(   res_blocked,   grouping_column = "group",   table_columns   = c("uniprot_id","protein_info"),   title_column    = "uniprot_id",   output_dir      = "10B_Blocked_dupCor",   tmp_subdir      = "tmp",   height          = 1000,   width           = 1000 )

previous_headings: 10. Paired / Blocking Designs (v1.0 Functions) > 10.2 Blocking with internal duplicateCorrelation
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: duplicateCorrelation() estimates average within-pair/within-subject correlation passes lmFit() residual variance accounts dependence. Great repeated measures many features (omics), full mixed model heavy. fit_limma_model() prints estimated intra-block correlation (e.g., 0.12). ’s low (< 0.05), function issues yellow message suggesting simple model may suffice. approach efficient large omics datasets conceptually similar random-intercept mixed model. provides benefit modeling correlation within subjects without heavy computation lme4 dream.

previous_headings: 10. Paired / Blocking Designs (v1.0 Functions) > 10.2 Blocking with internal duplicateCorrelation
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: Use paired fixed-effect pair complete want strict within-pair comparisons (pair gets coefficient). Use blocking (duplicateCorrelation) scalability expect common within-block correlation across features; conceptually similar random intercept.

what: Checks full rank, condition number, non-estimable contrasts, and (if using blocking) prints the duplicateCorrelation estimate. It works for either path:
title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: paired_fe (paired fixed-effect model) blocked (blocking random_factor)
code:
## ---- diagnostics_model_checks, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE ------- # Helper to print a clean header .hdr <- function(x) cat("
", paste0("## ", x), "
", sep="")  # Choose which object to diagnose (set one that exists) obj <- if (exists("blocked")) blocked else if (exists("paired_fe")) paired_fe else NULL stopifnot(!is.null(obj))  X <- obj$design$design_matrix C <- obj$design$contrast_matrix  .hdr("Design matrix summary") cat("Dimensions (n x p):", paste(dim(X), collapse=" x "), "
") cat("Column names:
"); print(colnames(X))  # Full-rank / rank deficiency check qrX   <- qr(X) rankX <- qrX$rank p     <- ncol(X) cat("QR rank:", rankX, "  |  Columns:", p, "
") if (rankX < p) {   cat("WARNING: design is NOT full rank. Check factor coding / redundant columns.
") } else {   cat("OK: design is full rank.
") }  # Condition number as a rough collinearity diagnostic k <- tryCatch(kappa(X), error = function(e) NA_real_) cat("Condition number (kappa):", if (is.na(k)) "NA" else sprintf("%.2f", k), "
") if (!is.na(k) && k > 1e6) cat("NOTE: Very high kappa suggests near-collinearity.
")  # Contrast estimability .hdr("Contrast estimability (limma::nonEstimable)") if (!is.null(C)) {   ne <- limma::nonEstimable(C, X)   if (is.null(ne)) {     cat("All contrasts appear estimable given the design.
")   } else {     cat("Non-estimable (or suspect) contrasts detected:
")     print(ne)   } } else {   cat("No contrast matrix present.
") }  # If using blocking via random_factor, show duplicateCorrelation estimate .hdr("Blocking / duplicateCorrelation diagnostic") if (!is.null(obj$design$random_factor)) {   blk_col <- obj$design$random_factor   stopifnot(blk_col %in% colnames(obj$metadata))   block_vec <- obj$metadata[, blk_col, drop = TRUE]   dc <- limma::duplicateCorrelation(object = obj$data, design = X, block = block_vec)   cat("Estimated intra-block correlation (duplicateCorrelation):",       sprintf("%.3f", dc$consensus.correlation), "
") } else {   cat("No random_factor set; duplicateCorrelation not applied.
") }  # Quick peek at coefficients and residual df if already fitted .hdr("Fit overview (if available)") if (!is.null(obj$eBayes_fit)) {   efit <- obj$eBayes_fit   cat("Coefficients matrix dims:", paste(dim(efit$coefficients), collapse=" x "), "
")   if (!is.null(efit$df.residual)) cat("Residual df (median across features):",                                       stats::median(efit$df.residual, na.rm = TRUE), "
")   # Show first few coefficient names   cat("Coefficient names:
"); print(colnames(efit$coefficients)) } else {   cat("Model not fitted yet (no eBayes_fit found in object).
") }

title: proteoDA_v1.0_workflow
text: Full rank: uses qr() compare rank vs. number columns; warns deficient. Collinearity: reports kappa(X) (large values ~ near-collinearity). Estimability: uses limma::nonEstimable() flag contrasts aren’t estimable design. Blocking: obj$design$random_factor set, re-computes prints duplicateCorrelation consensus correlation (purely diagnostic). Fit summary: ’ve already run fit_limma_model(), prints coefficient dimensions, residual df, coefficient names.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: Proteomics data shaped mixture biological signal technical variation introduced sample preparation, instrument drift, ionization efficiency, missingness patterns. Mass spectrometry intensities often show: Zeros missing values (non-detects) Global scale differences samples (loading / instrument drift) Intensity-dependent biases (curved M–plots) Heteroscedasticity (variance depends mean) differential abundance analysis, crucial : Handle zeros missing values Select evaluate normalization method Decide whether imputation required proteoDA v2.0 provides structured workflow built-diagnostics help users make transparent normalization choices.

what: Why zero values must be treated carefully
title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: proteomics pipelines, zeros often represent non-detected features rather true zero abundance. Treating zeros real values can: Artificially depress group means Inflate variance Produce exaggerated fold-changes

previous_headings: Handling Missing Values and Zeros > Why zero values must be treated carefully
title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: default, limma treats NA values expression matrix missing data. protein, lmFit(): Treats NA missing values Excludes missing samples per protein fits linear model using available intensities Compute correct residual degrees freedom eBayes() Proteins can therefore different numbers samples. entire group missing, coefficient/contrast becomes non-estimable limma returns NA logFC p-values. eBayes() uses correct residual degrees freedom protein. contrast, numeric zeros treated real values. zeros input matrix actually represent “detected” “detection limit”, including true intensities can: Pull group means toward zero Inflate within-group variance Create exaggerated fold-changes one group many zeros Thus, preferable convert zeros NA modeling.

previous_headings: Handling Missing Values and Zeros > Why zero values must be treated carefully
title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: proteoDA provides two helpers: zero_to_missing() — convert zeros (thresholded values) NA normalization modeling missing_to_zero() — optional post-analysis conversion. Used downstream tool requires fully numeric matrix, PCA.

what: Overview of Normalization Strategies in proteoDA v2.0
title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: proteoDA implements eight complementary normalization approaches, building evaluation framework proteiNorm (Graw et al., 2020, ACS Omega 5:25625–25633). Normalization strategies use

previous_headings: Overview of Normalization Strategies in proteoDA v2.0
title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: Use guide early choices: Already normalized upstream (e.g., Log2 median)? → Set DAList$tags$norm_method <- “Log2_median” skip re-normalizing. Clear global scaling differences, strong intensity trend? → median mean. Many low-abundance proteins, strong mean–variance dependence, zeros/near-zeros? → vsn. Want identical empirical distributions; assume proteins unchanged? → quantile. Curved “smile/frown” patterns M–plots (intensity-dependent bias)? → cycloess (cyclic loess) rlr. Single run modest variation, mainly load differences? → gi (global intensity scaling). Highly distinct groups clear biological effects? → avoid quantile (may suppress biology)

what: Simulated Example: Detecting and Fixing Bias Using MA Plots
title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: make diagnostics concrete, simulate small proteomics-like dataset : 1000 proteins × 8 samples 2 conditions (4 vs 4) Mild true differential abundance ~10% proteins Nonlinear Intensity-dependent technical bias two samples compare: Log2-transformed data VSN Cyclic loess

previous_headings: Simulated Example: Detecting and Fixing Bias Using MA Plots
title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
code: set.seed(123)  n_prot  <- 1000 n_samp  <- 8 group   <- rep(c("A","B"), each = 4)  # base mean on log2 scale  mu      <- rnorm(n_prot, mean = 20, sd = 1.5)  # true DE: 10% up in group B  is_de   <- rbinom(n_prot, 1, 0.1) == 1 lfc     <- ifelse(is_de, rnorm(n_prot, 1, 0.2), 0)  # construct log2 intensities with group effect + noise  log2_mat <- sapply(seq_len(n_samp), function(j) { g <- group[j] mu + (g == "B") * lfc + rnorm(n_prot, sd = 0.2) }) colnames(log2_mat) <- paste0(group, "_", 1:4) rownames(log2_mat) <- paste0("prot", 1:n_prot)  # add intensity-dependent bias to first 2 samples (e.g. bad run)  bias_fun <- function(x) 0.5 * sin((x - mean(x)) / 2) log2_mat[,1] <- log2_mat[,1] + bias_fun(log2_mat[,1]) log2_mat[,2] <- log2_mat[,2] - bias_fun(log2_mat[,2])  # convert back to raw intensities for VSN  raw_mat <- 2^log2_mat

previous_headings: Simulated Example: Detecting and Fixing Bias Using MA Plots
title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
code: ma_plot <- function(mat, i = 1, j = 2, main = "") { x_i <- mat[, i] x_j <- mat[, j] A   <- 0.5 * (x_i + x_j) M   <- x_i - x_j df  <- data.frame(A = A, M = M)  ggplot(df, aes(x = A, y = M)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.7) + geom_smooth(se = FALSE, span = 0.7) + labs(x = "A = 0.5*(log2 xi + log2 xj)", y = "M = log2 xi - log2 xj", title = main) + theme_bw() }

what: Real Data Example: Within-Group vs Between-Group MA Plots
title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: understand normalization interacts biology dataset, useful visually compare: MA plot two samples biological group, MA plot one normal vs one cancer sample. two situations reveal different structures help guide normalization approach appropriate. Since now real data DAList_raw$data, can create MA plots directly. proteoDA assume data log2 default MA plots require log2 intensities Without log2, M–shapes become meaningless (M ≠ log ratio) code checks scale, transforms needed, prevents -Inf, produces valid MA plot.
code: # 1) Load DAList_raw from raw.rds (example shown) if not already loaded above # raw_file <- system.file("extdata", "raw.rds", package = "proteoDA") # DAList_raw <- readRDS(raw_file)  # Ensure log2 scale for real data expr_raw <- as.matrix(DAList_raw$data)  if (max(expr_raw, na.rm = TRUE) > 200) {   expr_log2 <- log2(expr_raw) } else {   expr_log2 <- expr_raw } expr_log2[!is.finite(expr_log2)] <- NA  # Identify sample columns (replace with your actual column names if needed) # 4-10 = normal # 11-17 = cancer s_within_1 <- colnames(expr_log2)[4]   # within-group example, sample 1 s_within_2 <- colnames(expr_log2)[5]   # within-group example, sample 2  s_between_1 <- colnames(expr_log2)[4]  # normal sample s_between_2 <- colnames(expr_log2)[11]  # cancer sample (example)  # Generate both MA plots p_within  <- ma_plot(expr_log2,                      i = which(colnames(expr_log2) == s_within_1),                      j = which(colnames(expr_log2) == s_within_2),                      main = paste("Within-group:", s_within_1, "vs", s_within_2))  p_between <- ma_plot(expr_log2,                      i = which(colnames(expr_log2) == s_between_1),                      j = which(colnames(expr_log2) == s_between_2),                      main = paste("Between-group:", s_between_1, "vs", s_between_2))  # Display side-by-side library(patchwork) p_within + p_between

what: How to run proteoDA normalize_data() now the best method has been evaluated
title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: proteoDA v2.0: DAList$data_per_contrast exists (e.g. filter_proteins_per_contrast()), normalization performed per contrast written DAList$data_per_contrast[[contrast]], leaving DAList$data unchanged. Otherwise normalization applied DAList$data. can pick method (often VSN cyclic loess) apply normalize_data(). Available methods include: log2, median, mean, vsn, quantile, cycloess, rlr, gi
code: # VSN DAList_norm <- normalize_data(         DAList_clean, norm_method   = "vsn", input_is_log2 = FALSE,   # if the data has been log2 transformed, set to TRUE use_per_contrast_if_available = TRUE  # did you run filter_proteins_per_contrasts?  )  # cyclic loess with group defined; default uses global method if group is not defined DAList_norm <- normalize_data(         DAList_clean, norm_method   = "cycloess", input_is_log2 = FALSE, groups        = DAList_clean$metadata$group,  # only used for cyclic loess for within group normalization,  use_per_contrast_if_available = TRUE )

title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: Missing values label-free proteomics often missing random (MNAR) due low-abundance peptides fall detection limit. proteoDA implements Perseus-like left-censoring strategy via perseus_impute() function, operates log2-transformed intensities.
code: # Perseus-style imputation on a normalized DAList imputed <- perseus_impute(   norm,   shift              = 1.8,   width              = 0.3,   robust             = TRUE,   min_obs_per_sample = 5,   seed               = 1,   save_before_after  = TRUE )

title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: sample (column) log2 intensity matrix: Let xijx_{ij} observed log2 intensity feature ii sample jj. location parameter μj\mu_j (mean median), scale parameter σj\sigma_j (standard deviation MAD). every missing entry sample, draw replacement value downshifted Gaussian: xijimp∼𝒩(μj−𝚜𝚑𝚒𝚏𝚝⋅σj,(𝚠𝚒𝚍𝚝𝚑⋅σj)2). x^{\text{imp}}_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}\left(\mu_j - \texttt{shift} \cdot \sigma_j,\; (\texttt{width} \cdot \sigma_j)^2\right). Rows missing samples remain NA (uninformative features). produces small “shoulder” imputed values low-intensity end distribution, mimicking undetected low-abundance peptides rather inventing high-intensity signals.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: main tuning parameters perseus_impute() : shift (default 1.8) Controls far imputed distribution shifted observed mean units sample’s standard deviation. Larger values → imputed values left (lower intensity). Smaller values → imputed values closer main signal region. width (default 0.3) Scales standard deviation imputed distribution:σimp,j=𝚠𝚒𝚍𝚝𝚑⋅σj. \sigma_{\text{imp},j} = \texttt{width} \cdot \sigma_j. Smaller values → narrow band imputed values. Larger values → imputed values spread . robust (default TRUE) Chooses whether estimate per-sample distribution using: TRUE: median MAD (robust outliers), FALSE: mean SD (classic Perseus behaviour). min_obs_per_sample (default 5) Minimum number observed (non-missing) log2 values required sample estimating μj\mu_j σj\sigma_j. sample fewer many observations, perseus_impute() falls back pooled distribution: μpooled\mu_{\text{pooled}}, σpooled\sigma_{\text{pooled}} computed observed values across samples. prevents unstable per-sample estimates column almost entirely missing. seed (default NULL) Optional random seed reproducible imputation. Set fixed integer (e.g. seed = 1) obtain identical imputed values across runs. save_before_after (default FALSE) TRUE input DAList, function stores contrast: before_log2: matrix log2 intensities imputation, after_log2: matrix imputation, imputed_mask: logical matrix indicating entries imputed. diagnostics written DAList$imputation_per_contrast[[contrast]] used plotting function . store_mask (default TRUE) Controls whether logical mask imputed entries stored. mainly useful advanced diagnostics downstream sensitivity analyses. ::: Important: perseus_impute() assumes input data log2-transformed. safety check, function issues warning maximum absolute value matrix large (e.g., > 1e3), typically indicates raw intensities rather log2 values. :::

title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: input DAList, perseus_impute() behaves follows: DAList$data_per_contrast exists contains matrices contrast, imputation performed per contrast imputed matrices written back DAList$data_per_contrast[[contrast]]. data_per_contrast absent, function falls back imputing global matrix DAList$data. cases, save_before_after = TRUE, diagnostic matrices stored : diagnostics visualized using write_perseus_imputation_plots() helper.
code: DAList$imputation_per_contrast[[contrast]]$before_log2 DAList$imputation_per_contrast[[contrast]]$after_log2 DAList$imputation_per_contrast[[contrast]]$imputed_mask

title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: verify Perseus-style imputation behaved expected, proteoDA provides write_perseus_imputation_plots(), creates faceted histograms comparing observed imputed log2 intensities sample. requested contrast, function: Retrieves before_log2 after_log2 fromDAList$imputation_per_contrast[[contrast]]. sample ID, intensity value, status (Observed vs Imputed). Draws histograms per sample, faceted across samples contrast.
code: plots <- write_perseus_imputation_plots(   DAList     = imputed,   out_dir    = "PerseusPlots",   contrasts  = NULL,   # or a subset of contrast names   samples    = NULL,   # or a subset of sample IDs   bins       = 30,   facet_ncol = 4,   overlay    = TRUE )  # List available contrasts for which imputation plots were generated names(plots)  # Inspect one contrast interactively plots[["M1Y1_vs_Ref"]] #or plots[[1]] # first contrast

title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: contrasts Character vector contrast names plot. Defaults contrasts imputation diagnostics. samples Optional subset sample columns (names indices). default, samples contrast shown. bins (default 50) Number histogram bins. Larger values give finer resolution noisier counts. facet_ncol (default 4) Number columns facet layout (many samples per row). overlay (default TRUE) TRUE: observed imputed values drawn panel different fill colors. FALSE: alternative stacking behaviour (implemented plot_perseus_imputation()). out_dir, width, height, dpi, device Control plots saved format (e.g. "png" "pdf"). Even writing disk, function invisibly returns list ggplot objects interactive inspection.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Normalization and Imputation
text: well-behaved imputations: blue histogram (Observed) represents main distribution log2 intensities (e.g., ~15–30). red histogram (Imputed) appears smaller bump lower end distribution (e.g., ~10–15), reflecting low-abundance, undetected features. imputed region clearly separated central bulk observed distribution. red blue overlap heavily, shift/width parameters may conservative. diagnostics complement normalization evaluation plots confirming : Imputation occurs normalized log2 intensities, Missing values replaced plausible low-intensity values rather unrealistic overly influential ones.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: Preprocessing critical first step proteomics differential abundance (DA) workflow. proteoDA, separate preprocessing (cleaning filtering) modeling (limma-based statistical analysis) can: Explicitly document samples proteins removed Apply different filtering rules groups vs contrast-specific analyses Track preprocessing decisions via DAList$tags slot reproducibility vignette focuses preprocessing functions operate DAList: Converting embedded zeros missing values Filtering samples using metadata Filtering proteins using annotation Filtering proteins using missingness patterns across groups Filtering proteins per contrast using contrasts file Normalization, imputation, limma designs/statistical thresholds covered separate vignettes: Overall workflow: vignette("proteoDA_v2.0_workflow", package = "proteoDA") Normalization imputation: vignette("proteoDA_v2.0_Norm_Impute", package = "proteoDA") Limma designs statistical thresholds: vignette("proteoDA_v2.0_design", package = "proteoDA") Typical proteoDA preprocessing flow: Import data → create DAList Convert zeros → zero_to_missing() Optional sample-level annotation-based filtering (filter_samples(), filter_proteins_by_annotation()) Protein-level missingness filtering (filter_proteins_by_group() filter_proteins_by_proportion()) Optional contrast-specific filtering (filter_proteins_per_contrast()) Normalize / impute → fit limma models See normalization vignette details available normalization strategies diagnostics: vignette("proteoDA_v2.0_Norm_Impute", package = "proteoDA") Write tables plots → write_limma_tables() write_limma_plots()

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: Preprocessing options proteoDA v2.0

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: preprocessing functions accept return DAList, allowing chain : functions also update DAList$tags decisions can inspected later.
code: library(proteoDA)  DAList_clean <- DAList_raw |>             zero_to_missing() |>             filter_samples(batch != "bad_batch") |>             filter_proteins_by_annotation(!grepl("KERATIN", protein_name)) |>             filter_proteins_by_group(                 min_reps       = 2,                 min_groups     = 2,                 grouping_column = "group" )

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: Embedded zeros often represent missing intensities rather true zero abundance. Statistical modeling proteoDA assumes missingness encoded NA. Typical usage: use Early pipeline, filtering missingness running limma time upstream tools encode absence 0 rather NA Replaces 0 values DAList$data NA Leaves metadata annotation unchanged Optionally records tag DAList$tags$zero_to_missing (implementation specific)
code: DAList <- zero_to_missing(DAList)

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: cases may need convert NA back 0, e.g., : Exporting tools handle NA, PCA. Re-using data pipelines assume zeros Typical usage: run limma zero-filled data; use zeros export visualization appropriate.
code: DAList_export <- missing_to_zero(DAList)

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: filter_samples() removes samples DAList based logical conditions evaluated DAList$metadata. Function recap condition expression using columns DAList$metadata Samples kept condition evaluates TRUE Example: remove specific sample Example: keep “control” “treated” groups Example: remove samples group Example: remove pre-2018 samples Behavior messages Updates DAList$metadata DAList$data keep selected samples (DAList$data columns re-subset remaining row names metadata) Prints many samples removed shows removed metadata Errors filter produces NA values (avoid silent mis-filtering)
code: DAList <- filter_samples(DAList,                   condition) DAList <- filter_samples(DAList,                   sample_ID != "Sample_001") DAList <- filter_samples(DAList,                           group %in% c("Control", "Treated")) DAList <- filter_samples(DAList,                          group != "Pool") DAList <- filter_samples(DAList,                       year >= 2018)

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: filter_proteins_by_annotation() removes proteins using logical conditions DAList$annotation. useful removing contaminants, keratins, decoys, spike-standards, etc. Function recap condition evaluated context DAList$annotation Proteins kept condition TRUE Example: remove single protein Example: remove keratins contaminants Example: remove proteins molecular weight < 30 kDa Behavior tags Updates DAList$annotation re-subsets DAList$data rows success, DAList$tags$filter_proteins_by_annotation updated condition call Errors condition evaluates NA row
code: # general command DAList <- filter_proteins_by_annotation(DAList,             condition) DAList <- filter_proteins_by_annotation(DAList,             uniprot_id != "P12345") # Remove any protein which contains "keratin" in the name: DAList <- DAList |>       filter_proteins_by_annotation(!grepl("KERATIN", protein_name)) |>       filter_proteins_by_annotation(contaminant_flag == FALSE) # Remove any protein with molecular weight < 30 kDa DAList <- filter_proteins_by_annotation(DAList,                                           molecular_weight > 30)

what: Protein-level filtering by missingness across groups
title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: DA analysis, typically require minimum level non-missing data per group. proteoDA provides two global strategies: counts (number replicates number groups) → filter_proteins_by_group() proportion (fraction samples per group) → filter_proteins_by_proportion() depend grouping column DAList$metadata (e.g. “group”).

previous_headings: Protein-level filtering by missingness across groups
title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: General Rule Thumb: Require least 2/3 biological replicates values least one group. Limma requires least 2 replicates power increases increased replicates due better estimation model variance. protein: Count many samples per group non-missing values Require least min_reps replicates within least min_groups groups Function recap Arguments min_reps (required) Minimum number non-missing samples within group protein considered present min_groups (required) Minimum number groups must meet min_reps grouping_column Column DAList$metadata group labels (default “group”) Example: strict filtering (missingness allowed) data contains 5 biological replicates 4 conditions “groups” Example: least one replicate groups Behavior diagnostics Checks min_reps larger smallest group size (otherwise errors informative message) Checks enough groups can meet min_reps satisfy min_groups Builds internal matrix non-missing counts per protein × group Returns filtered DAList logs parameters DAList$tags$filter_proteins_by_group
code: DAList_filtered <- filter_proteins_by_group(                 DAList,                 min_reps       = 2,                 min_groups     = 1,                 grouping_column = "group" ) DAList_strict <- filter_proteins_by_group(           DAList,           min_reps       = 5,     # group size           min_groups     = 4,     # all groups           grouping_column = "group" ) DAList_lax <- filter_proteins_by_group(               DAList,               min_reps       = 1,               min_groups     = 4,               grouping_column = "group" )

previous_headings: Protein-level filtering by missingness across groups
title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: group sizes imbalanced, may prefer specify proportion rather absolute number replicates. protein group: Compute group-specific thresholds = ceiling(group_size * min_prop) Require protein present least threshold samples group Function recap Arguments min_prop Minimum fraction samples within group must non-missing (must 0 1, e.g. 0.5, 0.66, 1.0) grouping_column Column DAList$metadata group labels Example: strict (missing) Example: moderate (≥50% samples per group) Behavior tags Computes group-specific thresholds via ceiling(n_group * min_prop) Requires groups meet thresholds protein kept Updates DAList$data, DAList$annotation, logs parameters DAList$tags$filter_proteins_by_proportion
code: DAList_prop <- filter_proteins_by_proportion(             DAList,             min_prop       = 0.66,             grouping_column = "group" ) DAList_all_present <- filter_proteins_by_proportion(         DAList,         min_prop       = 1,         grouping_column = "group" ) DAList_half <- filter_proteins_by_proportion(       DAList,       min_prop       = 0.5,       grouping_column = "group" )

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: Global filters may strict lenient contrasts, especially heterogeneous designs. filter_proteins_per_contrast() allows : Use raw DAList contrasts Apply per-contrast missingness rules based groups involved contrast Store results DAList$data_per_contrast DAList$annotation_per_contrast downstream limma runs

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: Concept Given: contrasts_file row looks like GroupA_vs_GroupB=GroupA - GroupB grouping_column metadata (e.g., “group”) contrast: Identify two groups contrast (e.g., “GroupA” “GroupB”) Count non-missing values per protein group Apply min_reps require_both_groups decide proteins keep Store contrast-specific data annotation Function recap Arguments DAList Input DAList raw pre-filtered data, metadata, annotation contrasts_file Path CSV lines like GroupA_vs_GroupB=GroupA - GroupB (header) min_reps Minimum replicates required per group contrast require_both_groupsTRUE: groups must meet min_repsFALSE: least one group must meet min_reps grouping_column Column metadata group labels Outputs bookkeeping success, function: Adds DAList$filtered_proteins_per_contrast (named list retained IDs per contrast) Adds DAList$data_per_contrast (named list contrast-specific data matrices) Adds DAList$annotation_per_contrast (named list contrast-specific annotation data frames) Adds DAList$tags$retention_summary (data frame Contrast Proteins_Retained) per-contrast slots used downstream functions run_filtered_limma_analysis() plotting/reporting functions operate per contrast. NOTE: use filtered_proteins_per_contrast() apply normalize_data(), normalized values added DAList$data_per_contrast DAList$data remain untouched. Example: heterogeneous design
code: DAList_filtered <- filter_proteins_per_contrast(         DAList           = DAList,         contrasts_file   = "data/contrasts.csv",         min_reps         = 2,         require_both_groups = TRUE,         grouping_column  = "group" ) DAList <- zero_to_missing(DAList)  DAList <- filter_proteins_per_contrast(         DAList             = DAList,         contrasts_file     = "design/contrasts.csv",         min_reps           = 2,         require_both_groups = FALSE,         grouping_column    = "group" )  DAList$tags$retention_summary

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
code: DAList_pp <- DAList_raw |>         zero_to_missing() |>         filter_samples(group %in% c("Control", "Treatment")) |>         filter_proteins_by_annotation(!grepl("KERATIN", protein_name)) |>         filter_proteins_by_group(             min_reps       = 2,             min_groups     = 2,             grouping_column = "group" )

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
code: DAList_pp <- DAList_raw |>     zero_to_missing() |>     filter_samples(!qc_fail) |>     filter_proteins_by_proportion(         min_prop       = 0.5,         grouping_column = "group" )

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
code: DAList_pp <- DAList_raw |>     zero_to_missing() |>     filter_proteins_by_annotation(!decoy) |>     filter_proteins_per_contrast(         DAList             = _,         contrasts_file     = "data/contrasts.csv",         min_reps           = 2,         require_both_groups = FALSE,         grouping_column    = "group" )

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: proteoDA, preprocessing separate, explicit step designed make downstream limma modeling robust transparent. key ideas vignette : Handle zeros early Use zero_to_missing() missingness consistently encoded NA filtering modeling. Filter sample level needed Use filter_samples() metadata-based conditions remove poor-quality, failed, --scope samples touching protein-level filters. Use annotation remove unwanted proteins Use filter_proteins_by_annotation() remove contaminants, keratins, decoys, spike-ins, proteins flagged annotation. Control missingness across groups filter_proteins_by_group() replicate-count–based thresholds (min_reps, min_groups) filter_proteins_by_proportion() group sizes imbalanced proportion-based rule (min_prop) easier reason . Apply contrast-specific rules designs heterogeneousfilter_proteins_per_contrast() allows tailor missingness thresholds contrast stores per-contrast data annotation DAList$data_per_contrast DAList$annotation_per_contrast. Track decisions via tags preprocessing functions write DAList$tags, making easier document filters applied parameters. Together, tools let build reproducible preprocessing pipeline matches experimental design, balances sensitivity robustness, feeds clean, well-documented inputs proteoDA modeling reporting functions.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: DIA/DDA proteomics workflows, yes: Upstream tools often encode non-detected values 0, NA, NaN proteoDA function beginning workflow finds blanks NAs replaces numeric 0 clear input data values included Downstream modeling proteoDA assumes missing values NA Running zero_to_missing() early ensures consistent missing value handling time might need input data already NA-coded want run limma using zero values specifically.

what: When should I filter samples vs proteins?
title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: Use filter_samples() : Poor-quality flagged samples Known batch failures Removing specific sample subgroups Use protein-level filters (filter_proteins_by_annotation, filter_proteins_by_group, filter_proteins_by_proportion, filter_proteins_per_contrast) : Contaminants, decoys, spike-ins Insufficient data density (missingness) practice, typically: Filter samples first apply protein-level filters based cleaned sample set

what: How strict should my missingness filters be?
title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: single rule, common patterns: filter_proteins_by_group(min_reps = 1, min_groups = n_groups) filter_proteins_by_proportion(min_prop = 0.5) filter_proteins_by_group(min_reps = 2 3, min_groups >= 2) Possibly combine imputation restricted filtered proteins recommend trying thresholds checking many proteins remain model diagnostics look (e.g., moving SD plots).

title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: Use contrast-specific filtering : - design includes heterogeneous groups many unused samples given contrast - contrasts fewer replicates per group need slightly different rule - groups missing values - want maximize proteins per contrast without globally -filtering experiment simple balanced, global filters may enough. QC missing value heatmap can help decide much filtering required.

what: How do preprocessing choices interact with normalization and imputation?
title: proteoDA v2.0 Preprocessing
text: typical (recommended) order: Import → create DAList zero_to_missing() Sample-level annotation-level filtering Protein-level missingness filtering (filter_proteins_by_group / filter_proteins_by_proportion / filter_proteins_per_contrast) Normalization (see normalization vignette) Imputation (desired, normalization) Fit limma models (see design/statistics vignettes) See normalization vignette details available normalization strategies diagnostics: vignette("proteoDA_v2.0_Norm_Impute", package = "proteoDA") choice limma design contrasts described detail : vignette("proteoDA_v2.0_design", package = "proteoDA") Consistency key: decide pipeline, keep sequence datasets document thresholds project reports.

title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
text: Quick Start runs proteoDA v2.0 DIANN protein quant data without Perseus-style imputation, writes per-contrast results, combined tables, interactive plots. Assumes proteoDA_params.R defines: input_quan, metadata, contrasts, anno_start, anno_end, design, group, p.val, logFC, bin_size, filt_min_reps, require_both_groups, stat_cols, DA_dir, DA_table_cols, DA_title_col, ctrl_proteins, project_name, author, author2.

title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
code: library(proteoDA) library(yaml)  # Common args from proteoDA_params.R common_args <- list( design_formula  = design, pval_thresh     = p.val, lfc_thresh      = logFC, adj_method      = "BH", binsize         = bin_size, plot_movingSD   = TRUE, contrasts_file  = contrasts )

what: 2. Read DIANN input and prepare IDS
title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
code: stopifnot(file.exists(input_quan)) x <- read.csv(input_quan, stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE)  if (!("Protein.Group" %in% colnames(x))) stop("Missing 'Protein.Group' in DIANN input.")  # Make uniprot_id from first entry of Protein.Group u <- strsplit(x$Protein.Group, ";", fixed = TRUE) uniprot_id <- vapply(u, function(z) if (length(z)) z[[1]] else NA_character_, "") x <- cbind(uniprot_id = uniprot_id, x)  # Numeric NAs -> 0; character NAs/blanks -> "0" for (nm in names(x)) {   col <- x[[nm]] if (is.numeric(col)) {   col[is.na(col)] <- 0 } else {   col[is.na(col) | col == ""] <- "0" }   x[[nm]] <- col }

title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
code: stopifnot(file.exists(metadata)) md <- read.csv(metadata, stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE)  if (!exists("anno_start") || !exists("anno_end")) stop("Define anno_start and anno_end in params.") anno <- x[, anno_start:anno_end, drop = FALSE]

title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
code: # Rename DIANN 'file' columns to 'sample' names where present req <- c("file","sample") mis <- req[!(req %in% colnames(md))] if (length(mis)) stop(paste("Metadata missing:", paste(mis, collapse=", ")))  rename_map <- setNames(md$sample, md$file) present <- intersect(names(rename_map), colnames(x)) if (length(present)) {   idx <- match(present, colnames(x))   colnames(x)[idx] <- rename_map[present] }  # Keep sample columns in data that appear in metadata keep <- colnames(x)[colnames(x) %in% md$sample] intensity <- x[, keep, drop = FALSE]  # Trim metadata to those samples; rownames = sample md <- md[md$sample %in% keep, , drop = FALSE] rownames(md) <- md$sample  # Auto-align and validate aln <- align_data_and_metadata(     data                = intensity,     metadata            = md,     sample_col          = "sample",     group_col           = "group",     prefer_group_blocks = FALSE,     strict              = FALSE ) aln$changes  # optional preview of any reordering  intensity <- aln$data md        <- aln$metadata

what: 5. Build DAList and convert zeros → NA
title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
code: raw <- DAList(   data       = intensity,   annotation = anno,   metadata   = md,   design     = NULL,   eBayes_fit = NULL,   results    = NULL,   tags       = NULL )  d0 <- zero_to_missing(raw)  # convert embedded zeros to NA for missing-aware ops table(d0$metadata$group)

title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
code: stopifnot(file.exists(contrasts)) flt <- filter_proteins_per_contrast(     DAList              = d0,     contrasts_file      = contrasts,     min_reps            = filt_min_reps,     require_both_groups = require_both_groups,     grouping_column     = group )  # Tag that we're using DIANN log2 normalized data norm <- flt norm$tags$norm_method <- "diann_quan"  # (Optional) quick counts to CSV summary_df <- data.frame(   contrast = names(flt$filtered_proteins_per_contrast),   filtered_proteins = vapply(flt$filtered_proteins_per_contrast, length, 1L) ) write.csv(summary_df, "filtered_protein_counts.csv", row.names = FALSE)

title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
code: set.seed(1) imp <- perseus_impute(     norm,     shift = 1.8,     width = 0.3,     robust = TRUE,     save_before_after = TRUE,     seed = 1 )  # Save imputation diagnostics  invisible(write_perseus_imputation_plots(     DAList     = imp,     out_dir    = "PerseusPlots",     bins       = 30,     facet_ncol = 4,     overlay    = TRUE,     width      = 7,     height     = 7,     dpi        = 300,     device     = "png" ))

title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
code: if (!exists("design")) stop("Define 'design' (e.g., ~0 + group) in params.") mf <- model.frame(design, data = norm$metadata, drop.unused.levels = TRUE) mm <- model.matrix(attr(mf, "terms"), data = mf)  cat("Columns:", ncol(mm), " Rank:", qr(mm)$rank, " Full rank?:", qr(mm)$rank == ncol(mm), "\n")

title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
code: common <- list(   design_formula = design,   pval_thresh    = p.val,   lfc_thresh     = logFC,   adj_method     = "BH",   binsize        = bin_size,   plot_movingSD  = TRUE,   contrasts_file = contrasts )  res_noimp <- do.call(run_filtered_limma_analysis, c(list(DAList = norm), common)) res_imp  <- do.call(run_filtered_limma_analysis, c(list(DAList = imp),  common))

title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
code: # Build statlists  sl_noimp <- build_statlist(DAList = res_noimp, stat_cols = stat_cols) sl_imp   <- build_statlist(DAList = res_imp,   stat_cols = stat_cols)  # Tables (CSV + Excel with per-contrast sheets) dir.create(DA_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE) write_limma_tables(     res_noimp,     output_dir          = DA_dir,     overwrite           = TRUE,     contrasts_subdir    = NULL,     summary_csv         = NULL,     combined_file_csv   = NULL,     spreadsheet_xlsx    = NULL,     add_filter          = TRUE,     color_palette       = NULL,     add_contrast_sheets = TRUE,      statlist            = sl_noimp )  dir.create("impute_out", showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE) write_limma_tables(     res_imp,     output_dir             = "impute_out",     overwrite              = TRUE,     contrasts_subdir       = NULL,     summary_csv            = NULL,     combined_file_csv      = NULL,     spreadsheet_xlsx       = NULL,     add_filter             = TRUE,     color_palette          = NULL,     add_contrast_sheets    = TRUE,     statlist               = sl_imp )  # Interactive per-contrast HTML + static images  write_limma_plots(     DAList           = res_noimp,     grouping_column  = group,     table_columns    = DA_table_cols,     title_column     = DA_title_col,     height           = 1000, width = 1000,     output_dir       = DA_dir, overwrite = TRUE,     control_proteins = ctrl_proteins,     highlight_by     = "uniprot_id",     image_formats    = c("pdf","png") )  write_limma_plots(     DAList           = res_imp,     grouping_column  = group,     table_columns    = DA_table_cols,     title_column     = DA_title_col,     height           = 1000, width = 1000,     output_dir       = "impute_out", overwrite = TRUE,     control_proteins = ctrl_proteins,     highlight_by     = "uniprot_id",     image_formats    = c("pdf","png") )

what: 11. Save DAList Objects for QC and Analysis Reports
title: proteoDA v2.0 - Quick Start
code: # Without imputation (NAs) saveRDS(res_noimp, "results_NA.rds")  # With Perseus imputation saveRDS(res_imp, "results_NA_imputed.rds")  # Also provide a zero-filled version for certain QC/PCA utilities that require no NAs results0 <- missing_to_zero(res_noimp) saveRDS(results0, "results.rds")  results0_imputed <- missing_to_zero(res_imp) saveRDS(results0_imputed, "results_imputed.rds")

what: Technical Appendix. Normalization metrics and methods

Normalization metrics Used write_norm_report() (PCV, PEV, PMAD, correlations, log2-ratios) appendix provides additional technical details normalization diagnostics used write_norm_report() proteoDA v2.0. definitions evaluation criteria based proteiNorm framework described Graw et al., 20201.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix
text: sample jj, total intensity Tj=∑=1pxij, T_j = \sum_{=1}^{p} x_{ij}, ii indexes proteins (features), xijx_{ij} (raw normalized) intensity protein ii sample jj, pp number proteins. Large differences across TjT_j indicate unequal global scaling technical loading bias. Effective normalization reduces differences (analogous total intensity plots proteiNorm).

text: PCA applied protein × sample matrix centering (optionally scaling) features. used visualize: clustering biological group, batch effects, sample outliers, proportion variance captured leading components. normalization successful, samples tend cluster biology rather batch, replicate groups become tighter. Strong batch separation large dispersion replicates normalization suggests normalization fully corrected non-biological variation.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix
text: define missingness indicator matrix Mij={1if xij=NA,0otherwise. M_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{} x_{ij} = \text{NA}, \\ 0 & \text{otherwise}. \end{cases} sample-clustered heatmap created applying hierarchical clustering rows (proteins) columns (samples). Visual patterns heatmap help infer: MAR (Missing Random): missingness diffuse obviously associated specific groups batches. MNAR (Missing Random): structured blocks missingness associated certain groups, batches, intensity ranges. patterns guide choice imputation strategy whether imputation appropriate .

title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix
text: protein ii within group gg, coefficient variation CVig=σigμig, CV_{ig} = \frac{\sigma_{ig}}{\mu_{ig}}, μig\mu_{ig} mean intensity protein ii across replicates group gg, σig\sigma_{ig} standard deviation across replicates. pooled coefficient variation (PCV) PCV=mediani,g(CVig). PCV = \text{median}_{,g}\left( CV_{ig} \right). Lower PCV implies better agreement among replicates given normalization method.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix
text: protein ii within group gg, Varig=σig2. Var_{ig} = \sigma_{ig}^2. pooled estimate variance PEV=mediani,g(Varig). PEV = \text{median}_{,g}(Var_{ig}). PEV sensitive changes scale introduced normalization complements PCV.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix
text: robust alternative variance uses median absolute deviation (MAD). protein ii group gg, MADig=median(|xij−median(xi⋅)|), MAD_{ig} = \text{median}\left( \left| x_{ij} - \text{median}(x_{\cdot}) \right| \right), xi⋅x_{\cdot} denotes intensities protein ii across samples group. pooled MAD PMAD=mediani,g(MADig). PMAD = \text{median}_{,g}(MAD_{ig}). PMAD robust outliers reflects typical spread replicate intensities. Lower PMAD reflects reduced spread replicate intensities improved consistency within groups.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix
text: samples jj kk within group gg, compute Pearson correlation rjk=cor(x⋅j,x⋅k), r_{jk} = \text{cor}(x_{\cdot j}, x_{\cdot k}), x⋅jx_{\cdot j} vector intensities sample jj across proteins. distribution intragroup correlations (j,k,gj,k,g) summarized per normalization method. High intragroup correlations indicate replicates similar normalization.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix
text: pairwise Pearson correlations samples computed form correlation matrix R=[rjk]. R = [r_{jk}]. Hierarchical clustering applied rows columns RR produce correlation heatmap. heatmap highlights: separation experimental groups, presence batch effects, increased similarity replicates normalization.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix
text: pair samples j≠kj \neq k, define LRijk=log2(xijxik). LR_{ijk} = \log_2\left(\frac{x_{ij}}{x_{ik}}\right). log2-ratio distributions pool pairwise sample comparisons proteins. well-behaved normalization absence strong global shifts, distribution log2-ratios : approximately centered zero, without strong skew toward - -regulation. Systematic deviation zero can indicate residual bias introduced removed normalization procedure.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix
text: section consolidates mathematical conceptual details normalization method implemented proteoDA evaluated write_norm_report(), evaluates eight normalization strategies, mirroring methods described Section 5.2 Graw et al., 2020.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix

what: Motivation: Proteomics data violate log-scale assumptions
title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix
text: Proteomics intensities often exhibit: Strong mean–variance dependence, especially among low-intensity proteins Large heteroscedasticity low end (small absolute signal → disproportionately large variance) Zero, near-zero, negative background-subtracted intensities Instrument detection limits distort ratios low-abundance proteins features cause classical log2 transformation behave poorly: log compresses high intensities explodes variance low end. Zero/negative intensities need artificial offsets truncation. Log-ratios produce “banana-shaped” intensity–ratio plots (see Figure 3b–3d page S100) :contentReferenceoaicite:2. Huber et al. demonstrated real intensity data follow simple multiplicative error model; instead, variance structure additive multiplicative components best approximated quadratic mean–variance relationship (Figure 2, page S98) :contentReferenceoaicite:3: Var(Y)=(c1μ+c2)2+c3. \mathrm{Var}(Y) = (c_1\,\mu + c_2)^2 + c_3. Proteomics data exhibit characteristic shape: high relative variance low intensities, flattening high range.

Key properties: large y,arsinh(+)≈log(2by), \mathrm{arsinh}(+ ) \approx \log(2by),  transformation behaves like log2 (scaling). small y, arsinh linear, logarithmic. prevents extreme ratios, avoids undefined log(0), stabilizes variance log2 fails. title: proteoDA v2.0 Technical Appendix

Thus, VSN’s piecewise behavior — approximately linear low intensities, log-like high intensities — matches biological instrumental error distribution. results : Stabilized variance across proteins → critical thousands low-abundance peptides inflate downstream variance metrics. Higher intragroup correlation (PCV/PEV/PMAD decrease) → shown consistently across datasets proteiNorm Huber et al.’s evaluations. Improved fold-change interpretability → especially one condition many low-intensity values. need adding pseudocounts → avoids arbitrary decisions offsets. Improved power differential abundance testing → VSN difference statistic (h) detects truly regulated features (Figure 4, pages S101–S102) log-ratios. Huber et al. show , across multiple platforms, VSN-transformed difference statistic uniformly achieves highest detection power compared log-ratios (Figure 4). aligns proteomics datasets low-intensity proteins contribute disproportionately false positives.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Design and Statistical Thresholds
text: Common hypotheses: Main effect treatment (averaged cell types) Treatment effect within one cell type (e.g., WT treated vs WT Sham) Pure interaction: “treatment effect different cell types?” proteoDA, convenient approach define combined labels metadata (e.g. WT_Sham, WT_treated, Mut_Sham, Mut_treated) use -intercept design: contrasts can written directly terms combined groups: WT_treated_vs_WT_Sham = WT_treated - WT_Sham Mut_treated_vs_Mut_Sham= Mut_treated - Mut_Sham Interaction = (Mut_treated - Mut_Sham) - (WT_treated - WT_Sham) run_filtered_limma_analysis() related plotting functions can display contrasts biologically intuitive way still mapping underlying factorial design.
code: DAList$metadata$group <- factor(paste(DAList$metadata$celltype, DAList$metadata$treatment, sep = "_")) design <- ~ 0 + group DAList <- add_design(DAList, design_formula = design)  colnames(DAList$design$design_matrix)  # "WT_Sham", "WT_treated", "Mut_Sham", "Mut_treated", ...

text: bare limma workflow, write: block variable acts approximate random effect capturing correlation repeated measures subject.
code: design <- model.matrix(~ 0 + group, data = metadata) block  <- metadata$patient_id  dupcor <- limma::duplicateCorrelation(expr, design, block = block) fit    <- limma::lmFit(expr, design, block = block, correlation = dupcor$consensus) fit2   <- limma::eBayes(limma::contrasts.fit(fit, contrasts))

text: proteoDA v2.0, wrapper fit_limma_model() supports pattern storing blocking factor design metadata: Key points: design formula usually remains ~ 0 + group. patient_id column added fixed effect; instead referenced via random_factor. Contrasts written usual using group columns (e.g., Post_vs_Pre = Post - Pre).
code: # (1) Store the blocking factor in the metadata   DAList$metadata$patient_id <- factor(DAList$metadata$patient_id)  # (2) Add design as usual (fixed effects)   DAList <- add_design(DAList, design_formula = ~ 0 + group)  # (3) Tell proteoDA to use duplicateCorrelation   DAList$design$random_factor <- "patient_id"  # (4) Fit the model   DAList <- fit_limma_model(DAList)

title: proteoDA v2.0 Design and Statistical Thresholds
text: rank deficient design matrix can cause non-estimable coefficients. rank less number columns, coefficients uniquely estimated. Common causes: - perfect confounding (e.g., group appears exactly one batch) - redundant columns (e.g., including ~ group ~ 0 + group)
code: X <- DAList$design$design_matrix  # Base R QR decomposition qr_X <- qr(X) qr_X$rank ncol(X)   # full column rank is ideal  # Optional: using Matrix::rankMatrix if available if (requireNamespace("Matrix", quietly = TRUE)) { Matrix::rankMatrix(X) }

what: Statistical thresholds: movingSD and logFC Z-scores
title: proteoDA v2.0 Design and Statistical Thresholds
text: Mass-spectrometry–based proteomics data exhibit intensity-dependent variance: Low-abundance proteins show much larger variability measured fold changes high-abundance proteins. arises : low-intensity peptides close detection limits, missing values ratio compression common, high-abundance peptides stable ion counts. tested significance using single global variance estimate: high-abundance proteins -penalized, low-abundance proteins -penalized, leading biased detection differential abundance. proteoDA addresses modeling intensity-dependent variance using: moving (rolling) standard deviation logFC, logFC Z-score, normalizes logFC local noise level.

previous_headings: Statistical thresholds: movingSD and logFC Z-scores
title: proteoDA v2.0 Design and Statistical Thresholds
text: contrast: Compute mean log2 intensity protein. Sort proteins lowest highest intensity. Compute rolling-window standard deviation logFC values: using window size (binsize) chosen automatically provided user, na.rm = TRUE missing coefficients disrupt curve. Assign protein movingSD value according intensity. Compute Z-score: Z-score protein computed scaling log fold change local intensity-dependent noise estimate: Zi=logFCimovingSDi Z_i = \frac{\mathrm{logFC}_i}{\mathrm{movingSD}_i} : logFCi\mathrm{logFC}_i estimated log2_2 fold change protein ii, movingSDi\mathrm{movingSD}_i rolling standard deviation logFC values among proteins similar intensity. yields contrast-specific variance model captures real behavior MS data: high variance low intensity low variance high intensity.

previous_headings: Statistical thresholds: movingSD and logFC Z-scores
title: proteoDA v2.0 Design and Statistical Thresholds
text: Rather relying arbitrary window size, proteoDA can automatically choose one: Candidate window sizes generated fractions total proteins (e.g., 1%, 2%, 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%), constrained : minimum = 40% proteins maximum = 20% total proteins candidate, proteoDA computes movingSD curves contrasts calculates coefficient variation (CV) moving SD: CV=σSDμSD \text{CV} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{SD}}}{\mu_{\text{SD}}} : σSD\sigma_{\text{SD}} standard deviation moving SD values across intensity range, μSD\mu_{\text{SD}} mean. Lower CV values indicate smoother, stable movingSD curves. optimal binsize defined : smallest binsize whose CV within 5% minimum CV. diagnostic plot (movingSD_binsize_CV.png) included QC report. ensures chosen binsize smooth biologically informative. 8.3 Example: spike-benchmark dataset (M1Y1/M2Y1/M3Y2 vs Ref) dataset includes controlled spike-mixtures Lou et 2022: Lou, R., Cao, Y., Li, S. et al. Benchmarking commonly used software suites analysis workflows DIa proteomics phosphoproteomics. Nat Commun 14, 94 (2023). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-35740-1 Across ~9,300 quantified proteins, proteoDA selected: Candidate binsizes: 93, 187, 467, 934, 1401, 1867 Auto-selected binsize: 1401 (CV = 0.139, min CV = 0.135) means: Larger windows provide smoother, stable variance curves. algorithm chooses smallest window performs nearly well smoothest one.

previous_headings: Statistical thresholds: movingSD and logFC Z-scores > Automatic binsize selection
title: proteoDA v2.0 Design and Statistical Thresholds
text: Interpretation decreasing CV indicates smoother variance estimation larger windows. “elbow” plateau region marks binsizes sufficiently stable. proteoDA selects smallest binsize near plateau.

previous_headings: Statistical thresholds: movingSD and logFC Z-scores > Automatic binsize selection
title: proteoDA v2.0 Design and Statistical Thresholds
text: contrast, proteoDA produces: Moving SD vs rank index (proteins sorted intensity). Moving SD vs mean log2 intensity (MD-style plot). M3Y2_vs_Ref = 3-fold change   M2Y1_vs_Ref = 2-fold change   M1Y1_vs_Ref = change   Across three contrasts, movingSD curves show: High variance low intensity, Decreasing variance increasing intensity, Similar shapes across spike-contrasts, indicating variance structure dominated measurement behavior rather biology.

previous_headings: Statistical thresholds: movingSD and logFC Z-scores
title: proteoDA v2.0 Design and Statistical Thresholds
text: Moving SD vs rank index Shows rolling SD intensity-sorted proteins. High movingSD low intensities reflects high noise. Gradual decrease toward higher intensities shows improved precision. Smooth, monotonic curves indicate well-behaved data appropriate normalization. Moving SD vs mean log2 intensity (MD-style) Places variance curve biological intensity scale. Good diagnosing issues like: sample imbalance normalization artifacts missing-value patterns outlier batches Irregularities curves can flag contrasts experiments need closer inspection.

previous_headings: Statistical thresholds: movingSD and logFC Z-scores
title: proteoDA v2.0 Design and Statistical Thresholds
text: movingSD computed, proteoDA standardizes logFC values: Zi=logFCimovingSDi Z_i = \frac{\text{logFC}_i}{\text{movingSD}_i} Z-scores express large protein’s fold change relative expected noise protein’s intensity. Interpretation: Z ≈ 0 → reliable change relative noise. |Z| > 1 → fold change larger typical local variation. |Z| > 3 → high-confidence differential abundance. spike-example: M1Y1_vs_Ref (change): Z-scores cluster near 0 (biology, expected). M2Y1_vs_Ref (2-fold change): moderate shifts Z 2-fold changes. M3Y2_vs_Ref (3-fold change): largest Z-scores, reflecting strong biological effects.

previous_headings: Statistical thresholds: movingSD and logFC Z-scores
title: proteoDA v2.0 Design and Statistical Thresholds
text: MovingSD Z-scores answer practical questions: 1, “protein really changing, just noisy?” → Z-scores account intensity-dependent variance. “don’t small fold changes low abundance reach significance?” → movingSD reveals elevated noise low intensity. “experiment behave expected?” → movingSD curves validate variance structure (e.g., spike-ins). “Can trust p-values volcano plots?” → intensity-aware variance modeling, thresholds p-values better reflect real MS behavior. Together, movingSD Z-scores provide biologically meaningful way evaluate protein-level differential abundance mass-spectrometry datasets.

title: proteoDA v2.0 Design and Statistical Thresholds
text: Goal: detect cell-type–specific treatment effects. Example contrasts: WT_treated_vs_WT_Sham = WT_treated - WT_Sham Mut_treated_vs_Mut_Sham = Mut_treated - Mut_Sham Interaction = (Mut_treated - Mut_Sham) - (WT_treated - WT_Sham)
